content.problemStatement:
Given an 2D matrix m×nm \times nm×n  , return all its elements in **spiral order**.

Function DeclarationFunction NamespiralOrderspiralOrderspiralOrder – This function returns the elements of a matrix in spiral order.

Parameters
matrixmatrixmatrix : A 2D array of integers with mmm rows and nnn columns.
Return Value
Returns an array containing all matrix elements in **spiral order**.
Constraints
1≤m,n≤101 \leq m, n \leq 101≤m,n≤10
−100≤matrix[i][j]≤100−100 \leq matrix[i][j] \leq 100−100≤matrix[i][j]≤100
The matrix is non-empty

Input Format

The first line of each test case contains two space-separated integers mmm and nnn — the number of rows and columns of the matrix.
The next mmm lines each contain nnn space-separated integers, representing the rows of the matrix.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line all elements of the matrix in **spiral order**, separated by spaces.
constraints: m * n <= 15 (to keep each test case small for the hidden tests)
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers m and n — the number of rows and columns of the matrix. The next m lines each contain n space‑separated integers representing the matrix rows.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing all matrix elements in spiral order, separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The matrix is traversed clockwise starting from the top‑left corner, yielding the sequence 1,2,3,6,9,8,7,4,5.
Spiral order of the 3×4 matrix is 2,4,6,8,16,24,22,20,18,10,12,14.

solution.approach:
Maintain four boundaries: top, bottom, left, right. Repeatedly traverse the current outer rectangle: left→right on the top row, top→bottom on the right column, right→left on the bottom row (if still valid), and bottom→top on the left column (if still valid). After each side, shrink the corresponding boundary. Continue until top > bottom or left > right. This visits every element exactly once.
Time Complexity: O(m·n) per test case because each element is processed once.
Space Complexity: O(1) extra besides the output list.
